speaker: interviewer
text: This is tape four of an interview with a survivor. Her name is Lisbeth Korn-- Kornreich. OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: We left off when we were able to come to the United States. Now we had-- finally, we had a visa and. Finally all the German papers were in order. Because they used to give you one that would expire in 30 days, and then something that would expire in three months, and in the meantime that one was expired. So it took seven months to get all that in order. But then finally this came. And now the question was transportation. Because our rolling stock was in service of the war time effort and even Germans couldn't travel. However, the Jewish community in the United States-- now I don't want to say it was the Joint Distribution Committee, because I'm not sure. But some organization managed to get one railroad car available for immigration for Jews from the greater German Reich, which in those days bordered on the Pyrenees on Spain. Germany extended through-- throughout Europe, practicality. And this entire region got one railroad car. And mother and  I were fortunate enough to be put on this. We were extremely crowded-- needless to say, of course-- because you pushed in as many as you could. And we couldn't travel in the daytime because the Germans realized that there were-- or heard that there were Jews in there. So they stormed the car because they couldn't travel. We had two SS men, two black uniformed guarding us. And the windows were covered, of course. So we spend the days-- and by then, it was June. It was hot-- on sidings. And then at night, we were put on some kind of a freight train that went in the general direction. And after five days, we were in the Pyrenees, in the [INAUDIBLE]. And there they opened the doors and we could get out. Except we could barely do that. Our ankles were as big as our thighs from sitting cramped the way we were. And [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] then we were taken to-- overnight to a hotel in Saint Sebastian, which was just wonderful, with showers and warm water and a real bed. And we got breakfast there. But before that-- and this was also a very strange moment. It impressed me. There were some very, very, refined dark suited gentlemen-- two or three-- who approached us and said, if you want to write postcards to your family back home and you don't have the money for postcards or postage, we'll be happy to do that so you can reassure them that you are safe now. But we have no way to feed our families. And you're going to be in a hotel where you get breakfast. And you might not eat all the rolls. And you might not use all the sugar. Would you be kind enough and bring some out to us and we'll provide the postcards. This was after the Spanish Civil War. Those with people who were on the wrong side. And in [INAUDIBLE], you could see still the damage that the war had done. This was a very sobering experience. When it comes to human suffering, we were not alone, one way or another. [CRYING] Well then we came to Lisbon. And our ship didn't sail because the export lines were no longer in service. That was June, 1941. And again, the American Jewish community came to the rescue. They charted a Portuguese troop ship. Now Portuguese were never great colonial masters and their troop ships were nothing to brag about. And again, it was a question of putting as many people on it as possible, which as it should be. Because the Portuguese were-- the people in Lisbon were a little annoyed with us. We were hanging around. And there was a lot of espionage going on and a lot of of-- well, let's not call it prostitution, but whatever else you want to call it. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] The immigrant ladies, some of them, were freer than the Portuguese ladies, who still had to have a chaperone when they went to the dentist. It was a different society. And so as many as had papers-- and the papers also had a certain validity and were running out-- were put on this Portuguese troop ship. And that Portuguese troop ship, overcrowded, sailed and sailed and sailed and sailed. And after nine days, we were nearing the United States and the news came over the radio. We had no radio, but there was one young girl who had a little thing going, with the radio man on the ship. And she came back with the news that Germany had declared war on Russia. And that, as the rumor spread, set up a real [SIGHS] panic. Because now, obviously, the United States had to enter the war officially. And obviously, they were going to turn us back, like they had done once before. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Fortunately, this didn't happen. We could land. We couldn't land, because we had-- they had not provided docking fees. So we still sailed around a little bit, till somehow we were able to get a pier in Staten Island. By then it was Saturday. And Saturday the immigration officials didn't work after 12. And the crew went on land. And there was no electricity, no heating, no sanitation, no nothing. And there were some who had been processed and had gone on land. But with M, you are pretty far down the alphabet, so the immigration officials hadn't gotten to us. And some who were so religious that they didn't want to leave the ship because it was Shabbat and they weren't going to move. And then there were those beyond M, who were brought to Ellis Island. And this was my first experience with the United States. And it was absolutely fabulous. Ellis Island was nothing like what is being shown today. It was much more-- you know, there were no bunks, really, on top of each other-- whatever else they show you now. It was bright. It was friendly. There were all kinds of volunteers. Women took pride in greeting the foreigners, the immigrants, in their native language. There were apples and oranges. Then we were lined up and we were counted and we were each given a piece of soap-- I mean real soap, real, real soap. We took showers. We slept in a big dormitory. Everything was clean. There were big dishes on the table. Help yourself, try this, eat more. Then you left the table, up to here, went in another room. We were counted again. You got an orange in your hand. Unbelievable. There were cribs for infants. And there were tables set up so those who stayed there for any length of time could sew their own frock or skirt or whatnot. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] We were most impressed. If everything in the United States was going to be like it is at Ellis Island, there is nothing to fear. My father, as I said, had been here already and had come to-- well, then that was Sunday. And then Monday, things started to work again, and we were being processed, and they got to M, and my father was there. And he took us on the subway, the elevated. And that was the first disappointment. Because from wherever we came from, Staten Island or what-- I don't know where they-- where we got on the elevated. And my father, at that time, had rented an apartment in Elmhurst in-- on Long Island.

speaker: interviewee
text: And-- so there we were on the elevated. And we look down on these ugly roofs, with all the machinery on top, these ugly backyards. And it's a very ugly sight. You know, I was going to see splendid skyscrapers. You don't see anything on the elevated, when you come the way we came. So where is that beautiful New York?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, soon enough we saw the beautiful New York. But anyway, that was a great disappointment that they had plain houses, two story houses. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Somehow or other-- I don't know, I wasn't stupid. And I had an American girlfriend in Vienna, all that. But somehow, it was a great disappointment. This was very plebeian or proletarian or whatever. [LAUGHS] And it was-- that was not promising. And then we came to Elmhurst, as I said, where my father had rented an apartment, a furnished apartment. He had a job by then and made $35 a week. And that was in '41. It was relatively sufficient. And he had stocked the refrigerator-- unbelievable treasures. Now, I was raised in a very comfortable house and food was never a problem until Hitler came. But then, for three years, I hadn't seen any of these things. And there it was, in the midst of everything. And then I was taken to have an ice cream soda. And of course, it was much too heavy and much too much too soon. [LAUGHS] I blew up like this. And after a while, I had to watch my weight because the milk was milk and the butter was butter. [LAUGHING] You know, my system wasn't used to it anymore.

speaker: interviewee
text: And my mother, who should have been just as appreciative and grateful as you can be when you are with your husband again, and you have a household again, started to cry. She wasn't the crying kind. [SNIFFLING] At my age, I'm putting my feet under a rented table? [LAUGHS] I want to have my own table.

speaker: interviewee
text: And so as soon as we possibly could, we went out and rented an unfurnished place and got things from Goodwill and from [? Gimble's ?] Basement and god knows, but it was ours. She was-- I thought we have it made, you know. You don't have to worry about furniture. It's all there. You know, I was a young thing who never had a household or a place of her own, you know. And to me this was all just luxurious. It was awful. It was not a luxury place. But my mother, who used to keep house, couldn't take it.

speaker: interviewee
text: And so we went up from there. And she was very talented, as I said. In Vienna, she couldn't do anything outside the family because people would think, what kind of a man is he? He can't support his home. And here she flourished. She did her designing. She got some good connections. Fortunately, the first thing she did was learn English and did very well.

speaker: interviewee
text: And when she passed away in '61, a very sick lady who had a heart condition for years and years and years, we suddenly got a check from New York University-- a refund for the class she had not taken. She went, sick as she was with her heart condition-- we lived in Jackson Heights-- took the subway and the bus and god knows what and went to New York University, two nights a week, to take courses in public speaking, in composition one, and in things of that sort. Somewhere I still have a little speech she-- a little research she did on American jazz, the blues and jazz and whatnot. And she was not going to be the old grandma with a babushka who can't speak with her grandchildren. She did amazingly well. My father had some hard times finding the right job. And in the end, the last few years, he was with a metal parts firm and he was the commercial part of it. The others were the engineers and technical owners. And he did exceedingly well. And most of the paintings you see here are not only my parents. They were bought here or on their trips to Europe, in Europe. But there is nothing here from what we used to have. And this was done because it was more important-- they saw a painting and they had to have it and they didn't have the money. So they put down a deposit to hold it. And then they would deny themselves until they had the money to get the painting. And then later on, of course, they had a little more money, so they could more easily, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now what we're going to do, Tom, is we're going to stop here and get into the photographs. How much time do we have left?

speaker: interviewer
text: 12 minutes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Hm?

speaker: interviewer
text: 12 minutes.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, so--

speaker: interviewee
text: This is the engagement picture of my parents. My mother was a beautiful woman and has a big head of hair.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the year?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was taken in 1910 I would think because they married in 1912 and I was born in 1915. 1910.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Vienna, Austria, where they both were born and--

speaker: interviewer
text: --and then maybe you can--

speaker: interviewee
text: I told you about my school. When we were 16 and 17, we put on George Bernard Shaw's And since this was a girl's school, all  roles were played by girls. And since I was one of the tallest and whatever other reason, I got the male lead. And here I am on the left. And there is the nice teacher, Professor [? Pakenie, ?] who taught us our English and who put this play on the stage. And we acted it out on a real stage in a real theater-- one of the highlights of our school years.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where and what year?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was in 1929, in Vienna, Austria. In 1931 I graduated from the [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH]. So I was ready to enter the university in the following fall. This is our graduating class, the teachers in the front, except for one student on the left. And these are all the girls you saw in costume and with stage makeup. And this is how they looked in real life. And now you know why I got the male lead. I am the one who is sticking out there in the back row, the tall one.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell us the date and the place.

speaker: interviewee
text: And this is 1931, the year of my high school graduation.

speaker: interviewer
text: In Vienna?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, everything takes place in Vienna, Austria.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I went to the university and I studied at the philosophical faculty. My main-- [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] I studied chemistry but I never became a chemist. But I did get an absolutorium from the University in Vienna in 1939, which was after Hitler had come to power. And all it says, really, is that I had registered there for all these many semesters. It was a piece of paper that I thought might come in handy as long as I had paid tuition. But I did not graduate. I never got my doctorate. Add the piece about the [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewee
text: When I got it, it was kind of a little adventure. Because Jews were not allowed on the university premises anymore. But I went anyhow. And I went to the registrar. And I said, I would like my absolutorium since I had registered for all these semesters. And she said, yes, of course. You are-- can you confirm that you are racially pure? And I said, I certainly can. Well, I am.

speaker: interviewer
text: That's right, you are.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's not what he had in mind, obviously. But so I got it. And soon as I got it, I left as fast as I could. And shortly after I came to this country, I met my husband-- my husband to be. There was this very interesting, nice looking gentleman who had lived in Palestine for 6 and 1/2 years, who was an architect, who was knowledgeable-- very knowledgeable in all kinds of ways. He was extremely well read. And he also impressed me because he knew the BMT from CBS and all this alphabet soup here, which was all new to me. And he was very courteous, very well-mannered. And he asked me out. And I thought, well, that was nice. And then he asked me again and I thought, maybe he just feels he should because he was at our house and had coffee and cake and so I said no. And then he was very insistent and called again. And then I figured, why-- just because I'm very interested in him doesn't mean I shouldn't see him again. I thought I didn't want to make myself too obvious. Well, it turned out that he was just as anxious as I was. [LAUGHS] And it was a very short courtship because he went into the army. And-- but we were very sure of each other and each other's love no matter what. And then, after a while, we decided to get married, though he knew he was going to go overseas. And we did. And I stayed with my parents, of course, until he came back. He was in the entire Philippine invasion from [INAUDIBLE] to Australia to all the rest of it. And that was in December '45. And then we really lived together, not like just a furlough in a hotel or something like that. We had a household. And there was breakfast and dinner. And it was a great adjustment for all of us. The sad thing is that in all this happiness there was great unhappiness. Because his nuc-- 40 of his nuclear family had not survived. He comes from a large family. And they all had a large family. And everybody was married and had children. And he was one of eight. And three survived-- and then four, including him. But till they were found, by dribs and and drabs, it was a long time. And then we helped those that we could to come over and they stayed with us, so it was still not our home. And I was getting on in years and wanted a family. And well, the ones that are alive come first. And then they came, and they got settled, and everybody succeeded, thanks God. Most of them are not with us anymore. And then Steve and I had our family, our wonderful two girls. And that's another story. Yeah, this is a--

speaker: interviewer
text: We'll get to that [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a wonderful, wonderful man. For my 50th birthday, I did two things. I got my official portrait. Here you see it. And I asked my husband to pay tuition for me, instead of any other present, so I could go back to school and so my children shouldn't think of me as the old European lady with an accent. So I should have an American education. And I went to American University in Washington DC. And after two years, I got my masters in German and applied linguistics. And it turns out that my children never thought of me as the dumb immigrant with an accent. But I'm still very happy that I did what I did. And I enjoyed it and I made a career out of it. I still have the accent. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Now this is the official portrait on my husband Steve's-- for my-- for his 70th birthday, which occurred in May 1983. And you see, starting at the left, my son-in-law, Doctor Daniel Foss. And on his lap is my granddaughter, Sarah, and who was born in '81. Next to him is our older daughter, Carole, who is a psychiatrist-- met him in medical school-- our pride and joy. And my husband who always smiles when he has his family around-- always did. He was always-- he was a very serious man. But he always smiled when he saw any of us. A lot of love in this man. Next to him is the little sailor boy, Aaron Foss. Both children belong to Dan and Carole, the older ones. They now live in Denver. And next to me is the younger daughter, Brenda, our pride and joy, and her husband, Mark. And they are both accountants. They had no children at the time. They were newlyweds. This is my husband at age 75 in California, visiting his older brother. This was taken in '94 at the bat mitzvah of Sarah. She's the second from the left. I'm in the center and flanked by my two sons-in-law-- in front of them, my two daughters, Carole and Brenda and assorted grandchildren. I have five grandchildren. They are all in this picture. All are my favorites. And in the spring of '96, we had Michael's bar mitzvah. He is in the center. And here is my entire family. And of course, Steve is missing. On the occasion of my 80th birthday, my daughters-- all my children arranged for a lovely dinner and invited all my friends to the celebration. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] And this is what they mounted on the board, little me, age three or four. And people came from far away to help me celebrate. And it was very beautiful. It's a lovely memory.
